rows.pali: 22.Idāni yehi appicchatāsantuṭṭhitādīhi guṇehi vuttappakārassa sīlassa vodānaṃ hoti, te guṇe sampādetuṃ yasmā samādinnasīlena yoginā dhutaṅgasamādānaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 1.Now, while a meditator is engaged in the pursuit of virtue, he should set about undertaking the ascetic practices in order to perfect those special qualities of fewness of wishes, contentment, etc., by which the virtue of the kind already described, is cleansed.

rows.pali: Evañhissa appicchatāsantuṭṭhitāsallekhapavivekāpacayavīriyārambhasubharatādiguṇasalilavikkhālitamalaṃ sīlañceva suparisuddhaṃ bhavissati, vatāni ca sampajjissanti.
rows.english: For when his virtue is thus washed clean of stains by the waters of such special qualities as fewness of wishes, contentment, effacement, seclusion, dispersal, energy, and modest needs, it will become quite purified; and his vows will succeed as well.

rows.pali: Iti anavajjasīlabbataguṇaparisuddhasabbasamācāro porāṇe ariyavaṃsattaye patiṭṭhāya catutthassa bhāvanārāmatāsaṅkhātassa ariyavaṃsassa adhigamāraho bhavissati.
rows.english: And– so, when his whole behaviour has been purified by the special quality of blameless virtue and vows and he has become established in the [first] three of the ancient Noble Ones’ heritages, he may become worthy to attain to the fourth called “delight in development” (A II 27).

rows.pali: Tasmā dhutaṅgakathaṃ ārabhissāma.
rows.english: We shall therefore begin the explanation of the ascetic practices.

rows.pali: Bhagavatā hi pariccattalokāmisānaṃ kāye ca jīvite ca anapekkhānaṃ anulomapaṭipadaṃyeva ārādhetukāmānaṃ kulaputtānaṃ terasadhutaṅgāni anuññātāni.
rows.english: 2.Thirteen kinds of ascetic practices have been allowed by the Blessed One to clansmen who have given up the things of the flesh and, regardless of body and life, are desirous of undertaking a practice in conformity [with their aim].

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ – paṃsukūlikaṅgaṃ, tecīvarikaṅgaṃ, piṇḍapātikaṅgaṃ, sapadānacārikaṅgaṃ, ekāsanikaṅgaṃ, pattapiṇḍikaṅgaṃ, khalupacchābhattikaṅgaṃ, āraññikaṅgaṃ, rukkhamūlikaṅgaṃ, abbhokāsikaṅgaṃ, sosānikaṅgaṃ, yathāsanthatikaṅgaṃ, nesajjikaṅganti.
rows.english: They are: i. the refuse-rag-wearer’s practice, ii. the triple-robe-wearer’s practice, iii. the alms-food-eater’s practice, iv. the house-to-house-seeker’s practice, v. the one-sessioner’s practice, vi. the bowl-food-eater’s practice, vii. the later-food-refuser’s practice, viii. the forest-dweller’s practice, ix. the tree-root-dweller’s practice, x. the open-air-dweller’s practice, xi. the charnel-ground-dweller’s practice, xii. the any-bed-user’s practice, xiii. the sitter’s practice.

rows.pali: Tattha –
rows.english: 3.Herein:

rows.pali: 23.Tattha atthatoti tāva rathikasusānasaṅkārakūṭādīnaṃ yattha katthaci paṃsūnaṃ upari ṭhitattā abbhuggataṭṭhena tesu tesu paṃsukūlamivāti paṃsukūlaṃ, atha vā paṃsu viya kucchitabhāvaṃ ulatīti paṃsukūlaṃ, kucchitabhāvaṃ gacchatīti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 4.1. Herein, as to meaning, in the first place. i. It is “refuse” (paṃsukūla) since, owing to its being found on refuse in any such place as a street, a charnel ground, or a midden, it belongs, as it were, to the refuse in the sense of being dumped in anyone of these places. Or alternatively: like refuse it gets to a vile state (PAÍSU viya KUcchitabhāvaṃ ULAti), thus it is “refuse” (paṃsukūla); it goes to a vile state, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Evaṃ laddhanibbacanassa paṃsukūlassa dhāraṇaṃ paṃsukūlaṃ, taṃ sīlamassāti paṃsukūliko.
rows.english: The wearing of a refuse-[rag], which has acquired its derivative name1 in this way, is “refuse-[rag- wearing]” (paṃsukūla). That is his habit, thus he is a “refuse-[rag-wear-]er” (paṃsukūlika).

rows.pali: Paṃsukūlikassa aṅgaṃ paṃsukūlikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: The practice (aṅga) of the refuse-[rag-wear-]er is the “refuse-[rag- wear-]er’s practice” (paṃsukūlikaṅga).

rows.pali: Aṅganti kāraṇaṃ vuccati.
rows.english: It is the action that is called the “practice.”

rows.pali: Tasmā yena samādānena so paṃsukūliko hoti, tassetaṃ adhivacananti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Therefore it should be understood as a term for that by undertaking which one becomes a refuse-[rag-wear-]er.

rows.pali: Eteneva nayena saṅghāṭiuttarāsaṅgaantaravāsakasaṅkhātaṃ ticīvaraṃ sīlamassāti tecīvariko.
rows.english: ii. In the same way, he has the habit of [wearing] the triple robe (ti-cīvara)—in other words, the cloak of patches, the upper garment, and the inner clothing— thus he is a “triple-robe-[wear-]er” (tecīvarika).

rows.pali: Tecīvarikassa aṅgaṃ tecīvarikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: His practice is called the “triple- robe-wearer’s practice.”

rows.pali: Bhikkhāsaṅkhātānaṃ pana āmisapiṇḍānaṃ pātoti piṇḍapāto, parehi dinnānaṃ piṇḍānaṃ patte nipatananti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 5.iii. The dropping (pāta) of the lumps (piṇḍa) of material sustenance (āmisa) called alms (bhikkhā) is “alms food” (piṇḍapāta); the falling (nipatana) into the bowl of lumps (piṇḍa) given by others, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Taṃ piṇḍapātaṃ uñchati taṃ taṃ kulaṃ upasaṅkamanto gavesatīti piṇḍapātiko.
rows.english: He gleans that alms food (that falling of lumps), he seeks it by approaching such and such a family, thus he is called an “alms-food [eat-]er” (piṇḍapātika).

rows.pali: Piṇḍāya vā patituṃ vatametassāti piṇḍapātī, patitunti carituṃ, piṇḍapātī eva piṇḍapātiko.
rows.english: Or his vow is to gather (patituṃ)2 the lump (piṇḍa), thus he is a “lump-gatherer” (piṇḍapātin). To “gather” is to wander for. A “lump-gatherer” (piṇḍapātin) is the same as an “alms-food-eater” (piṇḍapātika).

rows.pali: Piṇḍapātikassa aṅgaṃ piṇḍapātikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: The practice of the alms-food-eater is the “alms-food-eater’s practice.”

rows.pali: Dānaṃ vuccati avakhaṇḍanaṃ, apetaṃ dānatoti apadānaṃ, anavakhaṇḍananti attho.
rows.english: 6.iv. It is a hiatus (avakhaṇḍana) that is called a “gap” (dāna).3 It is removed (apeta) from a gap, thus it is called “gapless” (apadāna); the meaning is, it is without hiatus.

rows.pali: Saha apadānena sapadānaṃ, avakhaṇḍanarahitaṃ anugharanti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: It is together with (saha) what is gapless (apadāna), thus it is “with the gapless” (sapadāna); devoid of hiatus—from house to house—is what is meant.

rows.pali: Sapadānaṃ carituṃ idamassa sīlanti sapadānacārī, sapadānacārī eva sapadānacāriko.
rows.english: His habit is to wander on what-is-with-the-gapless, thus he is a “gapless wanderer” (sapadāna- cārin). A gapless wanderer is the same as a “house-to-house-seeker” (sapadāna- cārika).

rows.pali: Tassa aṅgaṃ sapadānacārikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: His practice is the “house-to-house-seeker’s practice.”

rows.pali: Ekāsane bhojanaṃ ekāsanaṃ, taṃ sīlamassāti ekāsaniko.
rows.english: 7.v. Eating in one session is “one-session.” He has that habit, thus he is a “one- sessioner.”

rows.pali: Tassa aṅgaṃ ekāsanikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: His practice is the “one-sessioner’s practice.”

rows.pali: Dutiyabhājanassa paṭikkhittattā kevalaṃ ekasmiṃyeva patte piṇḍo pattapiṇḍo.
rows.english: vi. Alms (piṇḍa) in one bowl (patta) only because of refusing a second vessel, is “bowl-alms” (patta-piṇḍa).

rows.pali: Idāni pattapiṇḍagahaṇe pattapiṇḍasaññaṃ katvā pattapiṇḍo sīlamassāti pattapiṇḍiko.
rows.english: Now, making “bowl alms” (patta-piṇḍa) the name for the taking of alms food in the bowl: bowl-alms-food is his habit, thus he is a “bowl- food-eater” (pattapiṇḍika).

rows.pali: Tassa aṅgaṃ pattapiṇḍikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: His practice is the “bowl-food-eater’s practice.”

rows.pali: Khalūti paṭisedhanatthe nipāto.
rows.english: 8.vii. “No” (khalu) is a particle in the sense of refusing.

rows.pali: Pavāritena satā pacchā laddhaṃ bhattaṃ pacchābhattaṃ nāma, tassa pacchābhattassa bhojanaṃ pacchābhattabhojanaṃ, tasmiṃ pacchābhattabhojane pacchābhattasaññaṃ katvā pacchābhattaṃ sīlamassāti pacchābhattiko.
rows.english: Food (bhatta) obtained later by one who has shown that he is satisfied is called “later-food” (pacchā-bhatta). The eating of that later food is “later-food-eating.” Making “later-food” (pacchā- bhatta) the name for that later-food-eating: later-food is his habit, thus he is a “later- food-[eat-]er” (pacchābhattika).

rows.pali: Na pacchābhattiko khalupacchābhattiko.
rows.english: Not a later-food-eater is a “no-later-food-[eat-]er” (khalu-pacchābhattika), [that is, a “later-food-refuser”].

rows.pali: Samādānavasena paṭikkhittātirittabhojanassetaṃ nāmaṃ.
rows.english: This is the name for one who as an undertaking refuses extra food.

rows.pali: Aṭṭhakathāyaṃ pana vuttaṃ khalūti eko sakuṇo.
rows.english: But it is said in the commentary4 “Khalu is a certain kind of bird.

rows.pali: So mukhena phalaṃ gahetvā tasmiṃ patite puna aññaṃ na khādati.
rows.english: When it has taken a fruit into its beak and that drops, it does not eat any more.

rows.pali: Tādiso ayanti khalupacchābhattiko.
rows.english: This [bhikkhu] is like that.” Thus he is “a later-food-refuser” (khalu-pacchā-bhattika).

rows.pali: Tassa aṅgaṃ khalupacchābhattikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: His practice is the “later-food-refuser’s practice.”

rows.pali: Araññe nivāso sīlamassāti āraññiko.
rows.english: 9.viii. His habit is dwelling in the forest, thus he is a “forest-dweller.”

rows.pali: Tassa aṅgaṃ āraññikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: His practice is the “forest-dweller’s practice.”

rows.pali: Rukkhamūle nivāso rukkhamūlaṃ, taṃ sīlamassāti rukkhamūliko.
rows.english: ix. Dwelling at the root of a tree is “tree-root-dwelling.” He has that habit, thus he is a “tree-root-dweller.”

rows.pali: Rukkhamūlikassa aṅgaṃ rukkhamūlikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: The practice of the tree-root-dweller is the “tree-root- dweller’s practice.”

rows.pali: Abbhokāsikasosānikaṅgesupi eseva nayo.
rows.english: x., xi. Likewise with the open-air-dweller and the charnel-ground-dweller.

rows.pali: Yadeva santhataṃ yathāsanthataṃ, idaṃ tuyhaṃ pāpuṇātīti evaṃ paṭhamaṃ uddiṭṭhasenāsanassetaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: 10. xii. Only what has been distributed (yad eva santhata) is “as distributed” (yathāsanthata). This is a term for the resting place first allotted thus “This one falls to you.”

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ yathāsanthate viharituṃ sīlamassāti yathāsanthatiko.
rows.english: He has the habit of dwelling in that as distributed, thus he is an “as- distributed-user” (yathāsanthatika), [that is, an “any-bed-user”].

rows.pali: Tassa aṅgaṃ yathāsanthatikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: His practice is the “any-bed-user’s practice.”

rows.pali: Sayanaṃ paṭikkhipitvā nisajjāya viharituṃ sīlamassāti nesajjiko.
rows.english: xiii. He has the habit of keeping to the sitting [posture when resting], refusing to lie down, thus he is a “sitter.”

rows.pali: Tassa aṅgaṃ nesajjikaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: His practice is the “sitter’s practice.”

rows.pali: Sabbāneva panetāni tena tena samādānena dhutakilesattā dhutassa bhikkhuno aṅgāni, kilesadhunanato vā dhutanti laddhavohāraṃ ñāṇaṃ aṅgaṃ etesanti dhutaṅgāni.
rows.english: 11.All these, however, are the practices (aṅga) of a bhikkhu who is ascetic (dhuta) because he has shaken off (dhuta) defilement by undertaking one or other of them. Or the knowledge that has got the name “ascetic” (dhuta) because it shakes off (dhunana) defilement is a practice (aṅga) belonging to these, thus they are “ascetic practices” (dhutaṅga).

rows.pali: Atha vā dhutāni ca tāni paṭipakkhaniddhunanato aṅgāni ca paṭipattiyātipi dhutaṅgāni.
rows.english: Or alternatively, they are ascetic (dhuta) because they shake off (niddhunana) opposition, and they are practices (aṅga) because they are a way (paṭipatti).

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāvettha atthato viññātabbo vinicchayo.
rows.english: This, firstly, is how the exposition should be known here as to meaning.

rows.pali: Sabbāneva panetāni samādānacetanālakkhaṇāni.
rows.english: 12.2.All of them have as their characteristic the volition of undertaking.

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ "yo samādiyati, so puggalo.
rows.english: For this is said [in the commentary]: “He who does the undertaking is a person.

rows.pali: Yena samādiyati, cittacetasikā ete dhammā.
rows.english: That whereby he does the undertaking is states of consciousness and consciousness- concomitants.

rows.pali: Yā samādānacetanā, taṃ dhutaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: The volition of the act of undertaking is the ascetic practice.

rows.pali: Yaṃ paṭikkhipati, taṃ vatthū"ti.
rows.english: What it rejects is the instance.”

rows.pali: Sabbāneva ca loluppaviddhaṃsanarasāni, nilloluppabhāvapaccupaṭṭhānāni appicchatādiariyadhammapadaṭṭhānāni.
rows.english: All have the function of eliminating cupidity, and they manifest themselves with the production of non-cupidity. For their proximate cause they have the noble states consisting of fewness of wishes, and so on.

rows.pali: Evamettha lakkhaṇādīhi veditabbo vinicchayo.
rows.english: This is how the exposition should be known as to characteristic, etc., here.

rows.pali: Samādānavidhānatotiādīsu pana pañcasu sabbāneva dhutaṅgāni dharamāne bhagavati bhagavatova santike samādātabbāni.
rows.english: 13. 3. As regards the five beginning with the undertaking and directions: during the Blessed One’s lifetime all ascetic practices should be undertaken in the Blessed One’s presence.

rows.pali: Parinibbute mahāsāvakassa santike.
rows.english: After his attainment of Nibbāna this should be done in the presence of a principal disciple.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ asati khīṇāsavassa, anāgāmissa, sakadāgāmissa, sotāpannassa, tipiṭakassa, dvipiṭakassa, ekapiṭakassa, ekasaṅgītikassa, aṭṭhakathācariyassa.
rows.english: When he is not available it should be done in the presence of one whose cankers are destroyed, of a non-returner, of a once-returner, of a stream-enterer, of one who knows the three Piṭakas, of one who knows two of the Piṭakas, of one who knows one of the Piṭakas, of one who knows one Collection,5 of a teacher of the Commentaries.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ asati dhutaṅgadharassa, tasmimpi asati cetiyaṅgaṇaṃ sammajjitvā ukkuṭikaṃ nisīditvā sammāsambuddhassa santike vadantena viya samādātabbāni, apica sayampi samādātuṃ vaṭṭati eva.
rows.english: When he is not available it should be done in the presence of an observer of an ascetic practice. When he is not available, then after one has swept out the shrine terrace they can be undertaken seated in a reverential posture as though pronouncing them in the Fully Enlightened One’s presence. Also it is permitted to undertake them by oneself.

rows.pali: Ettha ca cetiyapabbate dve bhātikattherānaṃ jeṭṭhakabhātu dhutaṅgappicchatāya vatthu kathetabbaṃ.
rows.english: And here should be told the story of the senior of the two brothers who were elders at Cetiyapabbata and their fewness of wishes with respect to the ascetic practices6 (M-a II 140).

rows.pali: Ayaṃ tāva sādhāraṇakathā.
rows.english: This, firstly, is what applies to all [the practices].

rows.pali: 24.Idāni ekekassa samādānavidhānappabhedabhedānisaṃse vaṇṇayissāma.
rows.english: 14.Now, we shall proceed to comment on the undertaking, directions, grade, breach and benefits, of each one [separately].

rows.pali: Paṃsukūlikaṅgaṃ tāva "gahapatidānacīvaraṃ paṭikkhipāmi, paṃsukūlikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesu dvīsu vacanesu aññatarena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: i. First, the refuse-rag-wearer’s practice is undertaken with one of these two statements: “I refuse robes given by householders” or “I undertake the refuse-rag- wearer’s practice.”

rows.pali: Idaṃ tāvettha samādānaṃ.
rows.english: This, firstly, is the undertaking.

rows.pali: Evaṃ samādinnadhutaṅgena pana tena sosānikaṃ, pāpaṇikaṃ, rathiyacoḷaṃ, saṅkāracoḷaṃ, sotthiyaṃ, nhānacoḷaṃ, titthacoḷaṃ, gatapaccāgataṃ, aggiḍaḍḍhaṃ, gokhāyitaṃ, upacikākhāyitaṃ, undūrakhāyitaṃ, antacchinnaṃ, dasācchinnaṃ, dhajāhaṭaṃ, thūpacīvaraṃ, samaṇacīvaraṃ, ābhisekikaṃ, iddhimayaṃ, panthikaṃ, vātāhaṭaṃ, devadattiyaṃ, sāmuddiyantietesu aññataraṃ cīvaraṃ gahetvā phāletvā dubbalaṭṭhānaṃ pahāya thiraṭṭhānāni dhovitvā cīvaraṃ katvā porāṇaṃ gahapaticīvaraṃ apanetvā paribhuñjitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 15.One who has done this should get a robe of one of the following kinds: one from a charnel ground, one from a shop, a cloth from a street, a cloth from a midden, one from a childbed, an ablution cloth, a cloth from a washing place, one worn going to and returning from [the charnel ground], one scorched by fire, one gnawed by cattle, one gnawed by ants, one gnawed by rats, one cut at the end, one cut at the edge, one carried as a flag, a robe from a shrine, an ascetic’s robe, one from a consecration, one produced by supernormal power, one from a highway, one borne by the wind, one presented by deities, one from the sea. Taking one of these robe cloths, he should tear off and throw away the weak parts, and then wash the sound parts and make up a robe. He can use it after getting rid of his old robe given by householders.

rows.pali: Tattha sosānikanti susāne patitakaṃ.
rows.english: 16.Herein, “one from a charnel ground” is one dropped on a charnel ground.

rows.pali: Pāpaṇikanti āpaṇadvāre patitakaṃ.
rows.english: “One from a shop” is one dropped at the door of a shop.

rows.pali: Rathiyacoḷanti puññatthikehi vātapānantarena rathikāya chaḍḍitacoḷakaṃ.
rows.english: “A cloth from a street” is a cloth thrown into a street from inside a window by those who seek merit.

rows.pali: Saṅkāracoḷanti saṅkāraṭṭhāne chaḍḍitacoḷakaṃ.
rows.english: “A cloth from a midden” is a cloth thrown onto a place for rubbish.

rows.pali: Sotthiyanti gabbhamalaṃ puñchitvā chaḍḍitavatthaṃ.
rows.english: “One from a childbed” is a cloth thrown away after wiping up the stains of childbirth with it.

rows.pali: Tissāmaccamātā kira satagghanakena vatthena gabbhamalaṃ puñchāpetvā paṃsukūlikā gaṇhissantīti tālaveḷimagge chaḍḍāpesi.
rows.english: The mother of Tissa the Minister, it seems, had the stains of childbirth wiped up with a cloth worth a hundred [pieces], and thinking, “The refuse-rag wearers will take it,” she had it thrown onto the Tālaveli Road.7

rows.pali: Bhikkhū jiṇṇakaṭṭhānatthameva gaṇhanti.
rows.english: Bhikkhus took it for the purpose of mending worn places.

rows.pali: Nhānacoḷanti yaṃ bhūtavejjehi sasīsaṃ nhāpitā kāḷakaṇṇicoḷanti chaḍḍetvā gacchanti.
rows.english: 17. “An ablution cloth” is one that people who are made by devil doctors to bathe themselves, including their heads, are accustomed to throw away as a “cloth of ill luck.”

rows.pali: Titthacoḷanti nhānatitthe chaḍḍitapilotikā.
rows.english: “A cloth from washing place” is rags thrown away at a washing place where bathing is done.

rows.pali: Gatapaccāgatanti yaṃ manussā susānaṃ gantvā paccāgatā nhatvā chaḍḍenti.
rows.english: “One worn going to and coming from” is one that people throw away after they have gone to a charnel ground and returned and bathed.

rows.pali: Aggiḍaḍḍhanti agginā ḍaḍḍhappadesaṃ.
rows.english: “One scorched by fire” is one partly scorched by fire;

rows.pali: Tañhi manussā chaḍḍenti.
rows.english: for people throw that away.

rows.pali: Gokhāyitādīni pākaṭāneva.
rows.english: “One gnawed by cattle,” etc., are obvious;

rows.pali: Tādisānipi hi manussā chaḍḍenti.
rows.english: for people throw away such as these too.

rows.pali: Dhajāhaṭanti nāvaṃ ārohantā dhajaṃ bandhitvā ārūhanti.
rows.english: “One carried as a flag”: Those who board a ship do so after hoisting a flag.

rows.pali: Taṃ tesaṃ dassanātikkame gahetuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: It is allowable to take this when they have gone out of sight.

rows.pali: Yampi yuddhabhūmiyaṃ dhajaṃ bandhitvā ṭhapitaṃ, taṃ dvinnampi senānaṃ gatakāle gahetuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: Also it is allowable, when the two armies have gone away, to take a flag that has been hoisted on a battlefield.

rows.pali: Thūpacīvaranti vammikaṃ parikkhipitvā balikammaṃ kataṃ.
rows.english: 18. “A robe from a shrine” is an offering made by draping a termite-mound [in cloth].

rows.pali: Samaṇacīvaranti bhikkhusantakaṃ.
rows.english: “An ascetic’s robe” is one belonging to a bhikkhu.

rows.pali: Ābhisekikanti rañño abhisekaṭṭhāne chaḍḍitacīvaraṃ.
rows.english: “One from a consecration” is one thrown away at the king’s consecration place.

rows.pali: Iddhimayanti ehibhikkhucīvaraṃ.
rows.english: “One produced by supernormal power” is a “come-bhikkhu” robe.8

rows.pali: Panthikanti antarāmagge patitakaṃ.
rows.english: “One from a highway” is one dropped in the middle of a road.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana sāmikānaṃ satisammosena patitaṃ, taṃ thokaṃ rakkhitvā gahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: But one dropped by the owner’s negligence should be taken only after waiting a while.

rows.pali: Vātāhaṭanti vātena paharitvā dūre pātitaṃ, taṃ pana sāmike apassantena gahetuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: “One borne by the wind” is one that falls a long way off, having been carried by the wind. It is allowable to take it if the owners are not in sight.

rows.pali: Devadattiyanti anuruddhattherassa viya devatāhi dinnakaṃ.
rows.english: “One presented by deities” is one given by deities like that given to the Elder Anuruddha (Dhp-a II 173–74).

rows.pali: Sāmuddiyanti samuddavīcīhi thale ussāritaṃ.
rows.english: “One from the sea” is one washed up on dry land by the sea waves.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana saṅghassa demāti dinnaṃ, coḷakabhikkhāya vā caramānehi laddhaṃ, na taṃ paṃsukūlaṃ.
rows.english: 19.One given thus “We give it to the Order” or got by those who go out for alms- cloth is not a refuse-rag.

rows.pali: Bhikkhudattiyepi yaṃ vassaggena gāhetvā vā dīyati, senāsanacīvaraṃ vā hoti, na taṃ paṃsukūlaṃ.
rows.english: And in the case of one presented by a bhikkhu, one given after it has been got [at a presentation of robes by householders] at the end of the Rains, or a “resting-place robe” [that is, one automatically supplied by a householder to the occupant of a certain resting place] is not a refuse-rag.

rows.pali: No gāhāpetvā dinnameva paṃsukūlaṃ.
rows.english: It is a refuse-rag only when given after not having been so obtained.

rows.pali: Tatrapi yaṃ dāyakehi bhikkhussa pādamūle nikkhittaṃ, tena pana bhikkhunā paṃsukūlikassa hatthe ṭhapetvā dinnaṃ, taṃ ekatosuddhikaṃ nāma.
rows.english: And herein, that placed by the donors at a bhikkhu’s feet but given by that bhikkhu to the refuse-rag wearer by placing it in his hand is called pure in one way.

rows.pali: Yaṃ bhikkhuno hatthe ṭhapetvā dinnaṃ, tena pana pādamūle ṭhapitaṃ, tampi ekatosuddhikaṃ.
rows.english: That given to a bhikkhu by placing it in his hand but placed by him at the [refuse-rag wearer’s] feet is also pure in one way.

rows.pali: Yaṃ bhikkhunopi pādamūle ṭhapitaṃ, tenāpi tatheva dinnaṃ, taṃ ubhatosuddhikaṃ.
rows.english: That which is both placed at a bhikkhu’s feet and then given by him in the same way is pure in both ways.

rows.pali: Yaṃ hatthe ṭhapetvā laddhaṃ, hattheyeva ṭhapitaṃ, taṃ anukkaṭṭhacīvaraṃ nāma.
rows.english: One obtained by being placed in the hand and [given by being] placed in the hand too is not a strict man’s robe.

rows.pali: Iti imaṃ paṃsukūlabhedaṃ ñatvā paṃsukūlikena cīvaraṃ paribhuñjitabbanti idamettha vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: So a refuse-rag wearer should use the robe after getting to know about the kinds of refuse-rags. These are the directions for it in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana pabhedo, tayo paṃsukūlikā ukkaṭṭho majjhimo mudūti.
rows.english: 20.The grades are these. There are three kinds of refuse-rag wearers: the strict, the medium, and the mild.

rows.pali: Tattha sosānikaṃyeva gaṇhanto ukkaṭṭho hoti.
rows.english: Herein, one who takes it only from a charnel ground is strict.

rows.pali: Pabbajitā gaṇhissantīti ṭhapitakaṃ gaṇhanto majjhimo.
rows.english: One who takes one left [by someone, thinking] “One gone forth will take it” is medium.

rows.pali: Pādamūle ṭhapetvā dinnakaṃ gaṇhanto mudūti.
rows.english: One who takes one given by being placed at his feet [by a bhikkhu] is mild.

rows.pali: Tesu yassa kassaci attano ruciyā gihidinnakaṃ sāditakkhaṇe dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: The moment anyone of these of his own choice or inclination agrees to [accept] a robe given by a householder, his ascetic practice is broken.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso, "paṃsukūlacīvaraṃ nissāya pabbajjā"ti (mahāva. 128) vacanato nissayānurūpapaṭipattisabbhāvo, paṭhame ariyavaṃse patiṭṭhānaṃ, ārakkhadukkhābhāvo, aparāyattavuttitā, corabhayena abhayatā, paribhogataṇhāya abhāvo, samaṇasāruppaparikkhāratā, "appāni ceva sulabhāni ca tāni ca anavajjānī"ti (a. ni. 4.27; itivu. 101) bhagavatā saṃvaṇṇitapaccayatā, pāsādikatā, appicchatādīnaṃ phalanipphatti, sammāpaṭipattiyā anubrūhanaṃ, pacchimāya janatāya diṭṭhānugatiāpādananti.
rows.english: 21.The benefits are these. He actually practices in conformity with the dependence, because of the words “The going forth by depending on the refuse-rag robe” (Vin I 58, 96); he is established in the first of the Noble Ones’ heritages (A II 27); there is no suffering due to protecting; he exists independent of others; there is no fear of robbers; there is no craving connected with use [of robes]; it is a requisite suitable for an ascetic; it is a requisite recommended by the Blessed One thus “valueless, easy to get, and blameless” (A II 26); it inspires confidence; it produces the fruits of fewness of wishes, etc.; the right way is cultivated; a good example is set9 to later generations.

rows.pali: 25.Tadanantaraṃ pana tecīvarikaṅgaṃ "catutthakacīvaraṃ paṭikkhipāmi, tecīvarikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 23.ii. Next there is the triple-robe-wearer’s practice. This is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse a fourth robe” or “I undertake the triple- robe-wearer’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana tecīvarikena cīvaradussaṃ labhitvā yāva aphāsukabhāvena kātuṃ vā na sakkoti, vicārakaṃ vā na labhati, sūciādīsu vāssa kiñci na sampajjati, tāva nikkhipitabbaṃ.
rows.english: When a triple-robe wearer has got cloth for a robe, he can put it by for as long as, owing to ill-health, he is unable to make it up, or for as long as he does not find a helper, or lacks a needle, etc.,

rows.pali: Nikkhittapaccayā doso natthi.
rows.english: and there is no fault in his putting it by.

rows.pali: Rajitakālato pana paṭṭhāya nikkhipituṃ na vaṭṭati, dhutaṅgacoro nāma hoti.
rows.english: But it is not allowed to put it by once it has been dyed. That is called cheating the ascetic practice.

rows.pali: Idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: These are the directions for it.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 24.This too has three grades.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭhena rajanakāle paṭhamaṃ antaravāsakaṃ vā uttarāsaṅgaṃ vā rajitvā taṃ nivāsetvā itaraṃ rajitabbaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict should, at the time of dyeing, first dye either the inner cloth or the upper garment, and having dyed it, he should wear that round the waist and dye the other.

rows.pali: Taṃ pārupitvā saṅghāṭi rajitabbā.
rows.english: Then he can put that on over the shoulder and dye the cloak of patches.

rows.pali: Saṅghāṭiṃ pana nivāsetuṃ na vaṭṭati.
rows.english: But he is not allowed to wear the cloak of patches round the waist.

rows.pali: Idamassa gāmantasenāsane vattaṃ.
rows.english: This is the duty when in an abode inside a village.

rows.pali: Āraññake pana dve ekato dhovitvā rajituṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: But it is allowable for him in the forest to wash and dye two together.

rows.pali: Yathā pana kañci disvā sakkoti kāsāvaṃ ākaḍḍhitvā uparikātuṃ, evaṃ āsanne ṭhāne nisīditabbaṃ.
rows.english: However, he should sit in a place near [to the robes] so that, if he sees anyone, he can pull a yellow cloth over himself.

rows.pali: Majjhimassa rajanasālāyaṃ rajanakāsāvaṃ nāma hoti, taṃ nivāsetvā vā pārupitvā vā rajanakammaṃ kātuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: But for the medium one there is a yellow cloth in the dyeing room for use while dyeing, and it is allowable for him to wear that [as an inner cloth] or to put it on [as an upper garment] in order to do the work of dyeing.

rows.pali: Mudukassa sabhāgabhikkhūnaṃ cīvarāni nivāsetvā vā pārupitvā vā rajanakammaṃ kātuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: For the mild one it is allowable to wear, or put on, the robes of bhikkhus who are in communion (i.e. not suspended, etc.) in order to do the work of dyeing.

rows.pali: Tatraṭṭhakapaccattharaṇampi tassa vaṭṭati.
rows.english: A bedspread that remains where it is10 is also allowable for him,

rows.pali: Pariharituṃ pana na vaṭṭati.
rows.english: but he must not take it about him.

rows.pali: Sabhāgabhikkhūnaṃ cīvarampi antarantarā paribhuñjituṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: And it is allowed for him to use from time to time the robes of bhikkhus who are in communion.

rows.pali: Dhutaṅgatecīvarikassa pana catutthaṃ vattamānaṃ aṃsakāsāvameva vaṭṭati.
rows.english: It is allowed to one who wears the triple robe as an ascetic practice to have a yellow shoulder-cloth too as a fourth;

rows.pali: Tañca kho vitthārato vidatthi, dīghato tihatthameva vaṭṭati.
rows.english: but it must be only a span wide and three hands long.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi catutthakacīvaraṃ sāditakkhaṇeyeva dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: The moment anyone of these three agrees to [accept] a fourth robe, his ascetic practice is broken.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃpanānisaṃso, tecīvariko bhikkhu santuṭṭho hoti kāyaparihārikena cīvarena.
rows.english: 25.The benefits are these. The bhikkhu who is a triple-robe wearer is content with the robe as a protection for the body.

rows.pali: Tenassa pakkhino viya samādāyeva gamanaṃ, appasamārambhatā, vatthasannidhiparivajjanaṃ, sallahukavuttitā, atirekacīvaraloluppappahānaṃ, kappiye mattakāritāya sallekhavuttitā, appicchatādīnaṃ phalanipphattīti evamādayo guṇā sampajjantīti.
rows.english: Hence he goes taking it with him as a bird does its wings (M I 180); and such special qualities as having few undertakings, avoidance of storage of cloth, a frugal existence, the abandoning of greed for many robes, living in effacement by observing moderation even in what is permitted, production of the fruits of fewness of wishes, etc., are perfected.

rows.pali: 26.Piṇḍapātikaṅgampi "atirekalābhaṃ paṭikkhipāmi, piṇḍapātikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 27. iii. The alms-food-eater’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse a supplementary [food] supply” or “I undertake the alms- food-eater’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana piṇḍapātikena "saṅghabhattaṃ, uddesabhattaṃ, nimantanabhattaṃ, salākabhattaṃ, pakkhikaṃ, uposathikaṃ, pāṭipadikaṃ, āgantukabhattaṃ, gamikabhattaṃ, gilānabhattaṃ, gilānupaṭṭhākabhattaṃ, vihārabhattaṃ, dhurabhattaṃ, vārakabhatta"nti etāni cuddasa bhattāni na sāditabbāni.
rows.english: Now, this alms-food eater should not accept the following fourteen kinds of meal: a meal offered to the Order, a meal offered to specified bhikkhus, an invitation, a meal given by a ticket, one each half-moon day, one each Uposatha day, one each first of the half-moon, a meal given for visitors, a meal for travellers, a meal for the sick, a meal for sick-nurses, a meal supplied to a [particular] residence, a meal given in a principal house,11 a meal given in turn.

rows.pali: Sace pana "saṅghabhattaṃ gaṇhathā"tiādinā nayena avatvā "amhākaṃ gehe saṅgho bhikkhaṃ gaṇhātu, tumhepi bhikkhaṃ gaṇhathā"ti vatvā dinnāni honti, tāni sādituṃ vaṭṭanti.
rows.english: If, instead of saying “Take a meal given to the Order”, [meals] are given saying “The Order is taking alms in our house; you may take alms too”, it is allowable to consent.

rows.pali: Saṅghato nirāmisasalākāpi vihāre pakkabhattampi vaṭṭatiyevāti idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: Tickets from the Order that are not for actual food,12 and also a meal cooked in a monastery, are allowable as well. These are the directions for it.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 28.This too has three grades.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭho puratopi pacchatopi āhaṭabhikkhaṃ gaṇhati, pattadvāre ṭhatvā pattaṃ gaṇhantānampi deti, paṭikkamanaṃ āharitvā dinnabhikkhampi gaṇhati, taṃ divasaṃ pana nisīditvā bhikkhaṃ na gaṇhati.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict takes alms brought both from before and from behind, and he gives the bowl to those who take it while he stands outside a door. He also takes alms brought to the refectory and given there. But he does not take alms by sitting [and waiting for it to be brought later] that day.

rows.pali: Majjhimo taṃ divasaṃ nisīditvāpi gaṇhati, svātanāya pana nādhivāseti.
rows.english: The medium one takes it as well by sitting [and waiting for it to be brought later] that day; but he does not consent to [its being brought] the next day.

rows.pali: Mudukosvātanāyapi punadivasāyapi bhikkhaṃ adhivāseti.
rows.english: The mild one consents to alms [being brought] on the next day and on the day after.

rows.pali: Te ubhopi serivihārasukhaṃ na labhanti, ukkaṭṭhova labhati.
rows.english: Both these last miss the joy of an independent life.

rows.pali: Ekasmiṃ kira gāme ariyavaṃso hoti, ukkaṭṭho itare āha – "āyāmāvuso, dhammasavanāyā"ti.
rows.english: There is, perhaps, a preaching on the Noble Ones’ heritages (A II 28) in some village. The strict one says to the others “Let us go, friends, and listen to the Dhamma.”

rows.pali: Tesu eko ekenamhi, bhante, manussena nisīdāpitoti āha.
rows.english: One of them says, “I have been made to sit [and wait] by a man, venerable sir,”

rows.pali: Aparo mayā, bhante, svātanāya ekassa bhikkhā adhivāsitāti.
rows.english: and the other, “I have consented to [receive] alms tomorrow, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ te ubho parihīnā.
rows.english: So they are both losers.

rows.pali: Itaro pātova piṇḍāya caritvā gantvā dhammarasaṃ paṭisaṃvedesi.
rows.english: The other wanders for alms in the morning and then he goes and savours the taste of the Dhamma.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi saṅghabhattādiatirekalābhaṃ sāditakkhaṇeva dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: The moment anyone of these three agrees to the extra gain consisting of a meal given to the Order, etc., his ascetic practice is broken.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso, "piṇḍiyālopabhojanaṃ nissāya pabbajjā"ti (a. ni. 4.27; itivu. 101) vacanato nissayānurūpapaṭipattisabbhāvo, dutiye ariyavaṃse patiṭṭhānaṃ, aparāyattavuttitā, "appāni ceva sulabhāni ca tāni ca anavajjānī"ti bhagavatā saṃvaṇṇitapaccayatā, kosajjanimmaddanatā, parisuddhājīvatā, sekhiyapaṭipattipūraṇaṃ, aparapositā, parānuggahakiriyā, mānappahānaṃ, rasataṇhānivāraṇaṃ, gaṇabhojanaparamparabhojanacārittasikkhāpadehi anāpattitā, appicchatādīnaṃ anulomavuttitā, sammāpaṭipattibrūhanaṃ, pacchimajanatānukampananti.
rows.english: 29.The benefits are these. He actually practices in conformity with the dependence because of the words “The going forth by depending on the eating of lumps of alms food” (Vin II 58, 96); he is established in the second of the Noble Ones’ heritages; his existence is independent of others; it is a requisite recommended by the Blessed One thus “Valueless, easy to get, blameless” (A II 26); idleness is eliminated; livelihood is purified; the practice of the minor training rule [of the Pātimokkha] is fulfilled; he is not maintained by another; he helps others; pride is abandoned; craving for tastes is checked; the training precepts about eating as a group, substituting one meal [invitation for another] (see Vinaya, Pācittiya 33 and Comy.), and good behaviour, are not contravened; his life conforms to [the principles of] fewness of wishes; he cultivates the right way; he has compassion for later generations.

rows.pali: Evarūpassa hi –
rows.english: Since it is said:

rows.pali: 27.Sapadānacārikaṅgampi"loluppacāraṃ paṭikkhipāmi, sapadānacārikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 31.iv. The house-to-house seeker’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements “I refuse a greedy alms round” or “I undertake the house-to-house seeker’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana sapadānacārikena gāmadvāre ṭhatvā parissayābhāvo sallakkhetabbo.
rows.english: Now, the house-to-house seeker should stop at the village gate and make sure that there is no danger.

rows.pali: Yassā racchāya vā gāme vā parissayo hoti, taṃ pahāya aññattha carituṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: If there is danger in any street or village, it is allowable to leave it out and wander for alms elsewhere.

rows.pali: Yasmiṃ gharadvāre vā racchāya vā gāme vā kiñci na labhati, agāmasaññaṃ katvā gantabbaṃ.
rows.english: When there is a house door or a street or a village where he [regularly] gets nothing at all, he can go [past it] not counting it as a village.

rows.pali: Yattha kiñci labhati, taṃ pahāya gantuṃ na vaṭṭati.
rows.english: But wherever he gets anything at all it is not allowed [subsequently] to go [past] there and leave it out.

rows.pali: Iminā ca bhikkhunā kālataraṃ pavisitabbaṃ, evañhi aphāsukaṭṭhānaṃ pahāya aññattha gantuṃ sakkhissati.
rows.english: This bhikkhu should enter the village early so that he will be able to leave out any inconvenient place and go elsewhere.

rows.pali: Sace panassa vihāre dānaṃ dentā antarāmagge vā āgacchantā manussā pattaṃ gahetvā piṇḍapātaṃ denti vaṭṭati.
rows.english: But if people who are giving a gift [of a meal] in a monastery or who are coming along the road take his bowl and give alms food, it is allowable.

rows.pali: Iminā ca maggaṃ gacchantenāpi bhikkhācāravelāyaṃ sampattagāmaṃ anatikkamitvā caritabbameva.
rows.english: And as this [bhikkhu] is going along the road, he should, when it is the time, wander for alms in any village he comes to and not pass it by.

rows.pali: Tattha alabhitvā vā thokaṃ labhitvā vā gāmapaṭipāṭiyā caritabbanti idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: If he gets nothing there or only a little, he should wander for alms in the next village in order.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: These are the directions for it. 32.This too has three grades.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭho purato āhaṭabhikkhampi pacchato āhaṭabhikkhampi paṭikkamanaṃ āharitvā diyyamānampi na gaṇhati, pattadvāre pana pattaṃ vissajjeti.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict does not take alms brought from before or brought from behind or brought to the refectory and given there. He hands over his bowl at a door, however;

rows.pali: Imasmiñhi dhutaṅge mahākassapattherena sadiso nāma natthi.
rows.english: for in this ascetic practice there is none equal to the Elder Mahā Kassapa,

rows.pali: Tassapi pattavissaṭṭhaṭṭhānameva paññāyati.
rows.english: yet an instance in which even he handed over his bowl is mentioned (see Ud 29).

rows.pali: Majjhimo purato vā pacchato vā āhaṭampi paṭikkamanaṃ āhaṭampi gaṇhati, pattadvārepi pattaṃ vissajjeti, na pana bhikkhaṃ āgamayamāno nisīdati.
rows.english: The medium one takes what is brought from before and from behind and what is brought to the refectory, and he hands over his bowl at a door. But he does not sit waiting for alms.

rows.pali: Evaṃ so ukkaṭṭhapiṇḍapātikassa anulometi.
rows.english: Thus he conforms to the rule of the strict alms-food eater.

rows.pali: Muduko taṃ divasaṃ nisīditvā āgameti.
rows.english: The mild one sits waiting [for alms to be brought] that day.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi loluppacāre uppannamatte dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: The ascetic practice of these three is broken as soon as the greedy alms round starts [by going only to the houses where good alms food is given].

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso, kulesu niccanavakatā, candūpamatā, kulamaccherappahānaṃ, samānukampitā, kulūpakādīnavābhāvo, avhānānabhinandanā, abhihārena anatthikatā, appicchatādīnaṃ anulomavuttitāti.
rows.english: 33.The benefits are these. He is always a stranger among families and is like the moon (S II 197); he abandons avarice about families; he is compassionate impartially; he avoids the dangers in being supported by a family; he does not delight in invitations; he does not hope for [meals] to be brought; his life conforms to [the principles of] fewness of wishes, and so on.

rows.pali: 28.Ekāsanikaṅgampi "nānāsanabhojanaṃ paṭikkhipāmi, ekāsanikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 35.v. The one-sessioner’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse eating in several sessions” or “I undertake the one-sessioner’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana ekāsanikena āsanasālāyaṃ nisīdantena therāsane anisīditvā "idaṃ mayhaṃ pāpuṇissatī"ti patirūpaṃ āsanaṃ sallakkhetvā nisīditabbaṃ.
rows.english: When the one-sessioner sits down in the sitting hall, instead of sitting on an elder’s seat, he should notice which seat is likely to fall to him and sit down on that.

rows.pali: Sacassa vippakate bhojane ācariyo vā upajjhāyo vā āgacchati, uṭṭhāya vattaṃ kātuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: If his teacher or preceptor arrives while the meal is still unfinished, it is allowable for him to get up and do the duties.

rows.pali: Tipiṭakacūḷābhayatthero panāha "āsanaṃ vā rakkheyya bhojanaṃ vā, ayañca vippakatabhojano, tasmā vattaṃ karotu, bhojanaṃ pana mā bhuñjatū"ti.
rows.english: But the Elder Tipiṭaka Cūla-Abhaya said: “He should either keep his seat [and finish his meal] or [if he gets up he should leave the rest of] his meal [in order not to break the ascetic practice]. And this is one whose meal is still unfinished; therefore let him do the duties, but in that case let him not eat the [rest of the] meal.”

rows.pali: Idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: These are the directions.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 36.This too has three grades.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭho appaṃ vā hotu bahu vā, yamhi bhojane hatthaṃ otāreti, tato aññaṃ gaṇhituṃ na labhati.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict may not take anything more than the food that he has laid his hand on whether it is little or much.

rows.pali: Sacepi manussā "therena na kiñci bhutta"nti sappiādīni āharanti, bhesajjatthameva vaṭṭanti, na āhāratthaṃ.
rows.english: And if people bring him ghee, etc., thinking “The elder has eaten nothing,” while these are allowable for the purpose of medicine, they are not so for the purpose of food.

rows.pali: Majjhimo yāva patte bhattaṃ na khīyati, tāva aññaṃ gaṇhituṃ labhati.
rows.english: The medium one may take more as long as the meal in the bowl is not exhausted;

rows.pali: Ayañhi bhojanapariyantiko nāma hoti.
rows.english: for he is called “one who stops when the food is finished.”

rows.pali: Muduko yāva āsanā na vuṭṭhāti tāva bhuñjituṃ labhati.
rows.english: The mild one may eat as long as he does not get up from his seat.

rows.pali: So hi udakapariyantiko vā hoti yāva pattadhovanaṃ na gaṇhāti tāva bhuñjanato, āsanapariyantiko vā yāva na vuṭṭhāti tāva bhuñjanato.
rows.english: He is either “one who stops with the water” because he eats until he takes [water for] washing the bowl, or “one who stops with the session” because he eats until he gets up.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi nānāsanabhojanaṃ bhuttakkhaṇe dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: The ascetic practice of these three is broken at the moment when food has been eaten at more than one session.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso, appābādhatā, appātaṅkatā, lahuṭṭhānaṃ, balaṃ, phāsuvihāro, anatirittapaccayā anāpatti, rasataṇhāvinodanaṃ appicchatādīnaṃ anulomavuttitāti.
rows.english: 37.The benefits are these. He has little affliction and little sickness; he has lightness, strength, and a happy life; there is no contravening [rules] about food that is not what is left over from a meal; craving for tastes is eliminated; his life conforms to the [principles of] fewness of wishes, and so on.

rows.pali: 29.Pattapiṇḍikaṅgampi"dutiyakabhājanaṃ paṭikkhipāmi, pattapiṇḍikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 39.vi. The bowl-food-eater’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse a second vessel” or “I undertake the bowl-food-eater’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana pattapiṇḍikena yāgupānakāle bhājane ṭhapetvā byañjane laddhe byañjanaṃ vā paṭhamaṃ khāditabbaṃ, yāgu vā pātabbā.
rows.english: When at the time of drinking rice gruel, the bowl-food eater gets curry that is put in a dish; he can first either eat the curry or drink the rice gruel.

rows.pali: Sace pana yāguyaṃ pakkhipati, pūtimacchakādimhi byañjane pakkhitte yāgu paṭikūlā hoti, appaṭikūlameva ca katvā bhuñjituṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: If he puts it in the rice gruel, the rice gruel becomes repulsive when a curry made with cured fish, etc., is put into it. So it is allowable [to do this] only in order to use it without making it repulsive.

rows.pali: Tasmā tathārūpaṃ byañjanaṃ sandhāya idaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Consequently this is said with reference to such curry as that.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana madhusakkarādikaṃ appaṭikūlaṃ hoti, taṃ pakkhipitabbaṃ.
rows.english: But what is unrepulsive, such as honey, sugar,13 etc., should be put into it.

rows.pali: Gaṇhantena ca pamāṇayuttameva gaṇhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And in taking it he should take the right amount.

rows.pali: Āmakasākaṃ hatthena gahetvā khādituṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: It is allowable to take green vegetables with the hand and eat them.

rows.pali: Tathā pana akatvā patteyeva pakkhipitabbaṃ.
rows.english: But unless he does that they should be put into the bowl.

rows.pali: Dutiyakabhājanassa pana paṭikkhittattā aññaṃ rukkhapaṇṇampi na vaṭṭatīti idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: Because a second vessel has been refused it is not allowable [to use] anything else, not even the leaf of a tree. These are its directions.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 40.This too has three grades.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭhassa aññatra ucchukhādanakālā kacavarampi chaḍḍetuṃ na vaṭṭati.
rows.english: Herein, for one who is strict, except at the time of eating sugarcane, it is not allowed [while eating] to throw rubbish away,

rows.pali: Odanapiṇḍamacchamaṃsapūvepi bhinditvā khādituṃ na vaṭṭati.
rows.english: and it is not allowed while eating to break up rice-lumps, fish, meat and cakes. [The rubbish should be thrown away and the rice-lumps, etc., broken up before starting to eat.]

rows.pali: Majjhimassa ekena hatthena bhinditvā khādituṃ vaṭṭati, hatthayogī nāmesa.
rows.english: The medium one is allowed to break them up with one hand while eating; and he is called a “hand ascetic.”

rows.pali: Muduko pana pattayogī nāma hoti, tassa yaṃ sakkā hoti patte pakkhipituṃ, taṃ sabbaṃ hatthena vā dantehi vā bhinditvā khādituṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: The mild one is called a “bowl ascetic”; anything that can be put into his bowl he is allowed, while eating, to break up, [that is, rice lumps, etc.,] with his hand or [such things as palm sugar, ginger, etc.,] with his teeth.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi dutiyakabhājanaṃ sāditakkhaṇe dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: The moment anyone of these three agrees to a second vessel his ascetic practice is broken.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso, nānārasataṇhāvinodanaṃ.
rows.english: 41.The benefits are these. Craving for variety of tastes is eliminated;

rows.pali: Atricchatāya pahānaṃ, āhāre payojanamattadassitā, thālakādipariharaṇakhedābhāvo, avikkhittabhojitā, appicchatādīnaṃ anulomavuttitāti.
rows.english: excessiveness of wishes is abandoned; he sees the purpose and the [right] amount in nutriment; he is not bothered with carrying saucers, etc., about; his life conforms to [the principles of] fewness of wishes and so on.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pattapiṇḍikaṅge samādānavidhānappabhedabhedānisaṃsavaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This is the commentary on the undertaking, directions, grades, breach, and benefits, in the case of the bowl-food-eater’s practice.

rows.pali: 30.Khalupacchābhattikaṅgampi "atirittabhojanaṃ paṭikkhipāmi, khalupacchābhattikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 43.vii. The later-food-refuser’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse additional food” or “I undertake the later-food-refuser’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana khalupacchābhattikena pavāretvā puna bhojanaṃ kappiyaṃ kāretvā na bhuñjitabbaṃ.
rows.english: Now, when that later-food refuser has shown that he is satisfied, he should not again have the food made allowable [by having it put into his hands according to the rule for bhikkhus] and eat it.

rows.pali: Idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: These are the directions for it.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 44.This too has three grades.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭho yasmā paṭhamapiṇḍe pavāraṇā nāma natthi, tasmiṃ pana ajjhohariyamāne aññaṃ paṭikkhipato hoti, tasmā evaṃ pavārito paṭhamapiṇḍaṃ ajjhoharitvā dutiyapiṇḍaṃ na bhuñjati.
rows.english: Herein, there is no showing that he has had enough with respect to the first lump, but there is when he refuses more while that is being swallowed. So when one who is strict has thus shown that he has had enough [with respect to the second lump], he does not eat the second lump after swallowing the first.

rows.pali: Majjhimo yasmiṃ bhojane pavārito, tadeva bhuñjati.
rows.english: The medium one eats also that food with respect to which he has shown that he has had enough.

rows.pali: Muduko pana yāva āsanā na vuṭṭhāti tāva bhuñjati.
rows.english: But the mild one goes on eating until he gets up from his seat.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi pavāritānaṃ kappiyaṃ kārāpetvā bhuttakkhaṇe dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: The moment any one of these three has eaten what has been made allowable [again] after he has shown that he has had enough, his ascetic practice is broken.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃpanānisaṃso, anatirittabhojanāpattiyā dūrabhāvo, odarikattābhāvo, nirāmisasannidhitā, puna pariyesanāya abhāvo, appicchatādīnaṃ anulomavuttitāti.
rows.english: 45.The benefits are these. One is far from committing an offence concerned with extra food; there is no overloading of the stomach; there is no keeping food back; there is no renewed search [for food]; he lives in conformity with [the principles of] fewness of wishes, and so on.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ khalupacchābhattikaṅge samādānavidhānappabhedabhedānisaṃsavaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This is the commentary on the undertaking, directions, grades, breach, and benefits, in the case of the later-food-refuser’s practice.

rows.pali: 31.Āraññikaṅgampi "gāmantasenāsanaṃ paṭikkhipāmi, āraññikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 47. viii. The forest-dweller’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse an abode in a village” or “I undertake the forest-dweller’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana āraññikena gāmantasenāsanaṃ pahāya araññe aruṇaṃ uṭṭhāpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 48.Now, that forest dweller must leave an abode in a village in order to meet the dawn in the forest.

rows.pali: Tattha saddhiṃ upacārena gāmoyeva gāmantasenāsanaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, a village abode is the village itself with its precincts.

rows.pali: Gāmo nāma yo koci ekakuṭiko vā anekakuṭiko vā parikkhitto vā aparikkhitto vā samanusso vā amanusso vā antamaso atirekacātumāsaniviṭṭho yo koci satthopi.
rows.english: A “village” may consist of one cottage or several cottages, it may be enclosed by a wall or not, have human inhabitants or not, and it can also be a caravan that is inhabited for more than four months.

rows.pali: Gāmūpacāro nāma parikkhittassa gāmassa sace anurādhapurasseva dve indakhīlā honti, abbhantarime indakhīle ṭhitassa thāmamajjhimassa purisassa leḍḍupāto.
rows.english: The “village precincts” cover the range of a stone thrown by a man of medium stature standing between the gate-posts of a walled village, if there are two gate-posts, as at Anurādhapura (cf. Vin III 46).

rows.pali: Tassa lakkhaṇaṃ yathā taruṇamanussā attano balaṃ dassentā bāhaṃ pasāretvā leḍḍuṃ khipanti, evaṃ khittassa leḍḍussa patanaṭṭhānabbhantaranti vinayadharā.
rows.english: The Vinaya experts say that this [stone’s throw] is characterized as up to the place where a thrown stone falls, as, for instance, when young men exercise their arms and throw stones in order to show off their strength.

rows.pali: Suttantikā pana kākanivāraṇaniyamena khittassāti vadanti.
rows.english: But the Suttanta experts say that it is up to where one thrown to scare crows normally falls.

rows.pali: Aparikkhittagāme yaṃ sabbapaccantimassa gharassa dvāre ṭhito mātugāmo bhājanena udakaṃ chaḍḍeti, tassa patanaṭṭhānaṃ gharūpacāro.
rows.english: In the case of an unwalled village, the house precinct is where the water falls when a woman standing in the door of the outermost house of all throws water from a basin.

rows.pali: Tato vuttanayena eko leḍḍupāto gāmo, dutiyo gāmūpacāro.
rows.english: Within a stone’s throw of the kind already described from that point is the village. Within a second stone’s throw is the village precinct.

rows.pali: Araññaṃ pana vinayapariyāye tāva "ṭhapetvā gāmañca gāmūpacārañca sabbametaṃ arañña"nti (pārā. 92) vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 49.“Forest,” according to the Vinaya method firstly, is described thus: “Except the village and its precincts, all is forest” (Vin III 46).

rows.pali: Abhidhammapariyāye "nikkhamitvā bahi indakhīlā, sabbametaṃ arañña"nti (vibha. 529) vuttaṃ.
rows.english: According to the Abhidhamma method it is described thus: “Having gone out beyond the boundary post, all that is forest” (Vibh 251; Paṭis I 176).

rows.pali: Imasmiṃ pana suttantikapariyāye "āraññakaṃ nāma senāsanaṃ pañcadhanusatikaṃ pacchima"nti idaṃ lakkhaṇaṃ.
rows.english: But according to the Suttanta method its characteristic is this: “A forest abode is five hundred bow-lengths distant” (Vin IV 183).

rows.pali: Taṃ āropitena ācariyadhanunā parikkhittassa gāmassa indakhīlato aparikkhittassa paṭhamaleḍḍupātato paṭṭhāya yāva vihāraparikkhepā minitvā vavatthapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: That should be defined by measuring it with a strung instructor’s bow from the gate-post of a walled village, or from the range of the first stone’s throw from an unwalled one, up to the monastery wall.

rows.pali: Sace pana vihāro aparikkhitto hoti, yaṃ sabbapaṭhamaṃ senāsanaṃ vā bhattasālā vā dhuvasannipātaṭṭhānaṃ vā bodhi vā cetiyaṃ vā dūre cepi senāsanato hoti, taṃ paricchedaṃ katvā minitabbanti vinayaṭṭhakathāsu vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 50.But if the monastery is not walled, it is said in the Vinaya commentaries, it should be measured by making the first dwelling of all the limit, or else the refectory or regular meeting place or Bodhi Tree or shrine, even if that is far from a dwelling [belonging to the monastery].

rows.pali: Majjhimaṭṭhakathāyaṃ pana vihārassapi gāmasseva upacāraṃ nīharitvā ubhinnaṃ leḍḍupātānaṃ antarā minitabbanti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But in the Majjhima commentary it is said that, omitting the precincts of the monastery and the village, the distance to be measured is that between where the two stones fall.

rows.pali: Idamettha pamāṇaṃ.
rows.english: This is the measure here.

rows.pali: Sacepi āsanne gāmo hoti, vihāre ṭhitehi mānusakānaṃ saddo suyyati, pabbatanadīādīhi pana antaritattā na sakkā ujuṃ gantuṃ.
rows.english: 51.Even if the village is close by and the sounds of men are audible to people in the monastery, still if it is not possible to go straight to it because of rocks, rivers, etc.,

rows.pali: Yo tassa pakatimaggo hoti, sacepi nāvāya sañcaritabbo, tena maggena pañcadhanusatikaṃ gahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: in between, the five hundred bow-lengths can be reckoned by that road even if one has to go by boat.

rows.pali: Yo pana āsannagāmassa aṅgasampādanatthaṃ tato tato maggaṃ pidahati, ayaṃ dhutaṅgacoro hoti.
rows.english: But anyone who blocks the path to the village here and there for the purpose of [lengthening it so as to be able to say that he is] taking up the practice is cheating the ascetic practice.

rows.pali: Sace pana āraññikassa bhikkhuno upajjhāyo vā ācariyo vā gilāno hoti, tena araññe sappāyaṃ alabhantena gāmantasenāsanaṃ netvā upaṭṭhātabbo.
rows.english: 52.If a forest-dwelling bhikkhu’s preceptor or teacher is ill and does not get what he needs in the forest, he should take him to a village abode and attend him there.

rows.pali: Kālasseva pana nikkhamitvā aṅgayuttaṭṭhāne aruṇaṃ uṭṭhāpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: But he should leave in time to meet the dawn in a place proper for the practice.

rows.pali: Sace aruṇuṭṭhānavelāyaṃ tesaṃ ābādho vaḍḍhati, tesaṃyeva kiccaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: If the affliction increases towards the time of dawn, he must attend him

rows.pali: Na dhutaṅgasuddhikena bhavitabbanti idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: and not bother about the purity of his ascetic practice. These are the directions.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 53.This too has three grades.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭhena sabbakālaṃ araññe aruṇaṃ uṭṭhāpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict must always meet the dawn in the forest.

rows.pali: Majjhimo cattāro vassike māse gāmante vasituṃ labhati.
rows.english: The medium one is allowed to live in a village for the four months of the Rains.

rows.pali: Muduko hemantikepi.
rows.english: And the mild one, for the winter months too.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi yathā paricchinne kāle araññato āgantvā gāmantasenāsane dhammassavanaṃ suṇantānaṃ aruṇe uṭṭhitepi dhutaṅgaṃ na bhijjati.
rows.english: If in the period defined any one of these three goes from the forest and hears the Dhamma in a village abode, his ascetic practice is not broken if he meets the dawn there,

rows.pali: Sutvā gacchantānaṃ antarāmagge uṭṭhitepi na bhijjati.
rows.english: nor is it broken if he meets it as he is on his way back after hearing [the Dhamma].

rows.pali: Sace pana uṭṭhitepi dhammakathike muhuttaṃ nipajjitvā gamissāmāti niddāyantānaṃ aruṇaṃ uṭṭhahati, attano vā ruciyā gāmantasenāsane aruṇaṃ uṭṭhapenti, dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjatīti ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: But if, when the preacher has got up, he thinks “We shall go after lying down awhile” and he meets the dawn while asleep or if of his own choice he meets the dawn while in a village abode, then his ascetic practice is broken. This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃpanānisaṃso, āraññiko bhikkhu araññasaññaṃ manasikaronto bhabbo aladdhaṃ vā samādhiṃ paṭiladdhuṃ laddhaṃ vā rakkhituṃ, satthāpissa attamano hoti.
rows.english: 54.The benefits are these. A forest-dwelling bhikkhu who has given attention to the perception of forest (see MN 121) can obtain hitherto unobtained concentration, or preserve that already obtained. And the Master is pleased with him,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "tenāhaṃ, nāgita, tassa bhikkhuno attamano homi araññavihārenā"ti (a. ni. 6.42; 8.86).
rows.english: according as it is said: “So, Nāgita, I am pleased with that bhikkhu’s dwelling in the forest” (A III 343).

rows.pali: Pantasenāsanavāsino cassa asappāyarūpādayo cittaṃ na vikkhipanti, vigatasantāso hoti, jīvitanikantiṃ jahati, pavivekasukharasaṃ assādeti, paṃsukūlikādibhāvopi cassa patirūpo hotīti.
rows.english: And when he lives in a remote abode his mind is not distracted by unsuitable visible objects, and so on. He is free from anxiety; he abandons attachment to life; he enjoys the taste of the bliss of seclusion, and the state of the refuse-rag wearer, etc., becomes him.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ āraññikaṅge samādānavidhānappabhedabhedānisaṃsavaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This is the commentary on the undertaking, directions, grades, breach, and benefits, in the case of the forest-dweller’s practice.

rows.pali: 32.Rukkhamūlikaṅgampi "channaṃ paṭikkhipāmi, rukkhamūlikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 56. ix. The tree-root-dweller’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse a roof” or “I undertake the tree-root-dweller’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana rukkhamūlikena sīmantarikarukkhaṃ, cetiyarukkhaṃ, niyyāsarukkhaṃ, phalarukkhaṃ, vaggulirukkhaṃ, susirarukkhaṃ, vihāramajjhe ṭhitarukkhanti ime rukkhe vivajjetvā vihārapaccante ṭhitarukkho gahetabboti idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: The tree-root dweller should avoid such trees as a tree near a frontier, a shrine tree, a gum tree, a fruit tree, a bats’ tree, a hollow tree, or a tree standing in the middle of a monastery. He can choose a tree standing on the outskirts of a monastery. These are the directions.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 57.This has three grades too.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭho yathārucitaṃ rukkhaṃ gahetvā paṭijaggāpetuṃ na labhati.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict is not allowed to have a tree that he has chosen tidied up.

rows.pali: Pādena paṇṇasaṭaṃ apanetvā vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: He can move the fallen leaves with his foot while dwelling there.

rows.pali: Majjhimo taṃ ṭhānaṃ sampattehiyeva paṭijaggāpetuṃ labhati.
rows.english: The medium one is allowed to get it tidied up by those who happen to come along.

rows.pali: Mudukena ārāmikasamaṇuddese pakkositvā sodhāpetvā samaṃ kārāpetvā vālukaṃ okirāpetvā pākāraparikkhepaṃ kārāpetvā dvāraṃ yojāpetvā vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: The mild one can take up residence there after summoning monastery attendants and novices and getting them to clear it up, level it, strew sand and make a fence round with a gate fixed in it.

rows.pali: Mahadivase pana rukkhamūlikena tattha anisīditvā aññattha paṭicchanne ṭhāne nisīditabbaṃ.
rows.english: On a special day, a tree-root dweller should sit in some concealed place elsewhere rather than there.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi channe vāsaṃ kappitakkhaṇe dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: The moment any one of these three makes his abode under a roof, his ascetic practice is broken.

rows.pali: Jānitvā channe aruṇaṃ uṭṭhāpitamatteti aṅguttarabhāṇakā.
rows.english: The reciters of the Aṅguttara say that it is broken as soon as he knowingly meets the dawn under a roof.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso, rukkhamūlasenāsanaṃ nissāya pabbajjāti (mahāva. 128) vacanato nissayānurūpapaṭipattisabbhāvo, appāni ceva sulabhāni ca tāni ca anavajjānīti (a. ni. 4.27; itivu. 101) bhagavatā saṃvaṇṇitapaccayatā, abhiṇhaṃ tarupaṇṇavikāradassanena aniccasaññāsamuṭṭhāpanatā, senāsanamaccherakammārāmatānaṃ abhāvo, devatāhi sahavāsitā, appicchatādīnaṃ anulomavuttitāti.
rows.english: 58.The benefits are these. He practices in conformity with the dependence, because of the words “The going forth by depending on the root of a tree as an abode” (Vin I 58, 96); it is a requisite recommended by the Blessed One thus “Valueless, easy to get, and blameless” (A II 26); perception of impermanence is aroused through seeing the continual alteration of young leaves; avarice about abodes and love of [building] work are absent; he dwells in the company of deities; he lives in conformity with [the principles of] fewness of wishes, and so on.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ rukkhamūlikaṅge samādānavidhānappabhedabhedānisaṃsavaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This is the commentary on the undertaking, directions, grades, breach, and benefits, in the case of the tree-root-dweller’s practice.

rows.pali: 33.Abbhokāsikaṅgampi"channañca rukkhamūlañca paṭikkhipāmi, abbhokāsikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 60. x. The open-air-dweller’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse a roof and a tree root” or “I undertake the open-air-dweller’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tassa pana abbhokāsikassa dhammassavanāya vā uposathatthāya vā uposathāgāraṃ pavisituṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: An open-air dweller is allowed to enter the Uposatha-house for the purpose of hearing the Dhamma or for the purpose of the Uposatha.

rows.pali: Sace paviṭṭhassa devo vassati, deve vassamāne anikkhamitvā vassūparame nikkhamitabbaṃ.
rows.english: If it rains while he is inside, he can go out when the rain is over instead of going out while it is still raining.

rows.pali: Bhojanasālaṃ vā aggisālaṃ vā pavisitvā vattaṃ kātuṃ, bhojanasālāya there bhikkhū bhattena āpucchituṃ, uddisantena vā uddisāpentena vā channaṃ pavisituṃ, bahi dunnikkhittāni mañcapīṭhādīni anto pavesetuñca vaṭṭati.
rows.english: He is allowed to enter the eating hall or the fire room in order to do the duties, or to go under a roof in order to ask elder bhikkhus in the eating hall about a meal, or when teaching and taking lessons, or to take beds, chairs, etc., inside that have been wrongly left outside.

rows.pali: Sace maggaṃ gacchantena vuḍḍhatarānaṃ parikkhāro gahito hoti, deve vassante maggamajjhe ṭhitaṃ sālaṃ pavisituṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: If he is going along a road with a requisite belonging to a senior and it rains, he is allowed to go into a wayside rest house.

rows.pali: Sace na kiñci gahitaṃ hoti, sālāya ṭhassāmīti vegena gantuṃ na vaṭṭati.
rows.english: If he has nothing with him, he is not allowed to hurry in order to get to a rest house;

rows.pali: Pakatigatiyā gantvā paviṭṭhena pana yāva vassūparamā ṭhatvā gantabbanti idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: but he can go at his normal pace and enter it and stay there as long as it rains. These are the directions for it.

rows.pali: Rukkhamūlikassāpi eseva nayo.
rows.english: And the same rule applies to the tree-root dweller too.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 61.This has three grades too.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭhassa rukkhaṃ vā pabbataṃ vā gehaṃ vā upanissāya vasituṃ na vaṭṭati.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict is not allowed to live near a tree or a rock or a house.

rows.pali: Abbhokāseyeva cīvarakuṭiṃ katvā vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: He should make a robe-tent right out in the open and live in that.

rows.pali: Majjhimassa rukkhapabbatagehāni upanissāya anto appavisitvā vasituṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: The medium one is allowed to live near a tree or a rock or a house so long as he is not covered by them.

rows.pali: Mudukassa acchannamariyādaṃ pabbhārampi sākhāmaṇḍapopi pīṭhapaṭopi khettarakkhakādīhi chaḍḍitā tatraṭṭhakakuṭikāpi vaṭṭatīti.
rows.english: The mild one is allowed these: a [rock] overhang without a drip-ledge cut in it,15 a hut of branches, cloth stiffened with paste, and a tent treated as a fixture, that has been left by field watchers, and so on.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi vāsatthāya channaṃ vā rukkhamūlaṃ vā paviṭṭhakkhaṇe dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: The moment any one of these three goes under a roof or to a tree root to dwell there, his ascetic practice is broken.

rows.pali: Jānitvā tattha aruṇaṃ uṭṭhāpitamatteti aṅguttarabhāṇakā.
rows.english: The reciters of the Aṅguttara say that it is broken as soon as he knowingly meets the dawn there.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this case.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso, āvāsapalibodhupacchedo, thinamiddhapanūdanaṃ, "migā viya asaṅgacārino, aniketā viharanti bhikkhavo"ti (saṃ. ni. 1.224) pasaṃsāya anurūpatā, nissaṅgatā, cātuddisatā, appicchatādīnaṃ anulomavuttitāti.
rows.english: 62.The benefits are these: the impediment of dwellings is severed; stiffness and torpor are expelled; his conduct deserves the praise “Like deer the bhikkhus live unattached and homeless” (S I 199); he is detached; he is [free to go in] any direction; he lives in conformity with [the principles of] fewness of wishes, and so on.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ abbhokāsikaṅge samādānavidhānappabhedabhedānisaṃsavaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This is the commentary on the undertaking, directions, grades, breach, and benefits, in the case of the open-air-dweller’s practice.

rows.pali: 34.Sosānikaṅgampi"na susānaṃ paṭikkhipāmi, sosānikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 64.xi. The charnel-ground-dweller’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse what is not a charnel ground” or “I undertake the charnel- ground-dweller’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana sosānikena yaṃ manussā gāmaṃ nivesantā "idaṃ susāna"nti vavatthapenti, na tattha vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: Now, the charnel-ground dweller should not live in some place just because the people who built the village have called it “the charnel ground”

rows.pali: Na hi matasarīre ajjhāpite taṃ susānaṃ nāma hoti, jhāpitakālato pana paṭṭhāya sacepi dvādasavassāni chaḍḍitaṃ, taṃ susānameva.
rows.english: for it is not a charnel ground unless a dead body has been burnt on it. But as soon as one has been burnt on it, it becomes a charnel ground. And even if it has been neglected for a dozen years, it is so still.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ pana vasantena caṅkamamaṇḍapādīni kāretvā mañcapīṭhaṃ paññapetvā pānīyaparibhojanīyaṃ upaṭṭhāpetvā dhammaṃ vācentena na vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 65. One who dwells there should not be the sort of person who gets walks, pavilions, etc., built, has beds and chairs set out and drinking and washing water kept ready, and preaches Dhamma;

rows.pali: Garukaṃ hi idaṃ dhutaṅgaṃ, tasmā uppannaparissayavighātatthāya saṅghattheraṃ vā rājayuttakaṃ vā jānāpetvā appamattena vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: for this ascetic practice is a momentous thing. Whoever goes to live there should be diligent. And he should first inform the senior elder of the Order or the king’s local representative in order to prevent trouble.

rows.pali: Caṅkamantena addhakkhikena āḷāhanaṃ olokentena caṅkamitabbaṃ.
rows.english: When he walks up and down, he should do so looking at the pyre with half an eye.

rows.pali: Susānaṃ gacchantenāpi mahāpathā ukkamma uppathamaggena gantabbaṃ.
rows.english: On his way to the charnel ground he should avoid the main roads and take a by-path.

rows.pali: Divāyeva ārammaṇaṃ vavatthapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: He should define all the objects [there] while it is day,

rows.pali: Evañhissa taṃ rattiṃ bhayānakaṃ na bhavissati, amanussā rattiṃ viravitvā viravitvā āhiṇḍantāpi na kenaci paharitabbā.
rows.english: so that they will not assume frightening shapes for him at night. Even if non-human beings wander about screeching, he must not hit them with anything.

rows.pali: Ekadivasampi susānaṃ agantuṃ na vaṭṭati.
rows.english: It is not allowed to miss going to the charnel ground even for a single day.

rows.pali: Majjhimayāmaṃ susāne khepetvā pacchimayāme paṭikkamituṃ vaṭṭatīti aṅguttarabhāṇakā.
rows.english: The reciters of the Aṅguttara say that after spending the middle watch in the charnel ground he is allowed to leave in the last watch.

rows.pali: Amanussānaṃ piyaṃ tilapiṭṭhamāsabhattamacchamaṃsakhīratelaguḷādikhajjabhojjaṃ na sevitabbaṃ.
rows.english: He should not take such foods as sesame flour, pease pudding, fish, meat, milk, oil, sugar, etc., which are liked by non-human beings.

rows.pali: Kulagehaṃ na pavisitabbanti idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: He should not enter the homes of families.16 These are the directions for it.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 66.This has three grades too.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭhena yattha dhuvaḍāhadhuvakuṇapadhuvarodanāni atthi, tattheva vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict should live where there are always burnings and corpses and mourning.

rows.pali: Majjhimassa tīsu ekasmimpi sati vaṭṭati.
rows.english: The medium one is allowed to live where there is one of these three.

rows.pali: Mudukassa vuttanayena susānalakkhaṇaṃ pattamatte vaṭṭati.
rows.english: The mild one is allowed to live in a place that possesses the bare characteristics of a charnel ground already stated.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi na susānamhi vāsaṃ kappanena dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: When any one of these three makes his abode in some place not a charnel ground, his ascetic practice is broken.

rows.pali: Susānaṃ agatadivaseti aṅguttarabhāṇakā.
rows.english: It is on the day on which he does not go to the charnel ground, the Aṅguttara reciters say.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this case.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso maraṇassatipaṭilābho, appamādavihāritā, asubhanimittādhigamo, kāmarāgavinodanaṃ, abhiṇhaṃ kāyasabhāvadassanaṃ, saṃvegabahulatā ārogyamadādippahānaṃ, bhayabheravasahanatā, amanussānaṃ garubhāvanīyatā, appicchatādīnaṃ anulomavuttitāti.
rows.english: 67.The benefits are these. He acquires mindfulness of death; he lives diligently; the sign of foulness is available (see Ch. VI); greed for sense desires is removed; he constantly sees the body’s true nature; he has a great sense of urgency; he abandons vanity of health, etc.; he vanquishes fear and dread (MN 4); non-human beings respect and honour him; he lives in conformity with [the principles of] fewness of wishes, and so on.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ sosānikaṅge samādānavidhānappabhedabhedānisaṃsavaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This is the commentary on the undertaking, directions, grades, breach, and benefits, in the case of the charnel-ground dweller’s practice.

rows.pali: 35.Yathāsanthatikaṅgampi"senāsanaloluppaṃ paṭikkhipāmi, yathāsanthatikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 69. xii. The any-bed-user’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse greed for resting places” or “I undertake the any-bed-user’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana yathāsanthatikena yadassa senāsanaṃ "idaṃ tuyhaṃ pāpuṇātī"ti gāhitaṃ hoti, teneva tuṭṭhabbaṃ, na añño uṭṭhāpetabbo.
rows.english: The any-bed user should be content with whatever resting place he gets thus: “This falls to your lot.” He must not make anyone else shift [from his bed].

rows.pali: Idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: These are the directions.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 70.This has three grades too.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭho attano pattasenāsanaṃ dūreti vā accāsanneti vā amanussadīghajātikādīhi upaddutanti vā uṇhanti vā sītalanti vā pucchituṃ na labhati.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict is not allowed to ask about the resting place that has fallen to his lot: “Is it far? ” or “Is it too near? ” or “Is it infested by non-human beings, snakes, and so on? ” or “Is it hot? ” or “Is it cold? ”.

rows.pali: Majjhimo pucchituṃ labhati.
rows.english: The medium one is allowed to ask,

rows.pali: Gantvā pana oloketuṃ na labhati.
rows.english: but not to go and inspect it.

rows.pali: Muduko gantvā oloketvā sacassa taṃ na ruccati, aññaṃ gahetuṃ labhati.
rows.english: The mild one is allowed to inspect it and, if he does not like it, to choose another.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi senāsanaloluppe uppannamatte dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjatīti ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: As soon as greed for resting places arises in any one of these three, his ascetic practice is broken. This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso, "yaṃ laddhaṃ tena tuṭṭhabba"nti (jā. 1.1.136; pāci. 793) vuttovādakaraṇaṃ, sabrahmacārīnaṃ hitesitā, hīnapaṇītavikappapariccāgo, anurodhavirodhappahānaṃ, atricchatāya dvārapidahanaṃ, appicchatādīnaṃ anulomavuttitāti.
rows.english: 71.The benefits are these. The advice “He should be content with what he gets” (J-a I 476; Vin IV 259) is carried out; he regards the welfare of his fellows in the life of purity; he gives up caring about inferiority and superiority; approval and disapproval are abandoned; the door is closed against excessive wishes; he lives in conformity with [the principles] of fewness of wishes, and so on.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ yathāsanthatikaṅge samādānavidhānappabhedabhedānisaṃsavaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This is the commentary on the undertaking, directions, grades, breach, and benefits, in the case of the any-bed-user’s practice.

rows.pali: 36.Nesajjikaṅgampi"seyyaṃ paṭikkhipāmi, nesajjikaṅgaṃ samādiyāmī"ti imesaṃ aññataravacanena samādinnaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 73.xiii. The sitter’s practice is undertaken with one of the following statements: “I refuse lying down” or “I undertake the sitter’s practice.”

rows.pali: Tena pana nesajjikena rattiyā tīsu yāmesu ekaṃ yāmaṃ uṭṭhāya caṅkamitabbaṃ.
rows.english: The sitter can get up in any one of three watches of the night and walk up and down:

rows.pali: Iriyāpathesu hi nipajjitumeva na vaṭṭati.
rows.english: for lying down is the only posture not allowed.

rows.pali: Idamassa vidhānaṃ.
rows.english: These are the directions.

rows.pali: Pabhedato pana ayampi tividho hoti.
rows.english: 74.This has three grades too.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkaṭṭhassa neva apassenaṃ, na dussapallatthikā, na āyogapaṭṭo vaṭṭati.
rows.english: Herein, one who is strict is not allowed a back-rest or cloth band or binding-strap [to prevent falling while asleep].17

rows.pali: Majjhimassa imesu tīsu yaṃkiñci vaṭṭati.
rows.english: The medium one is allowed any one of these three.

rows.pali: Mudukassa apassenampi dussapallatthikāpi āyogapaṭṭopi bibbohanampi pañcaṅgopi sattaṅgopi vaṭṭati.
rows.english: The mild one is allowed a back-rest, a cloth band, a binding-strap, a cushion, a “five-limb” and a “seven-limb.”

rows.pali: Pañcaṅgo pana piṭṭhiapassayena saddhiṃ kato.
rows.english: A “five-limb” is [a chair] made with [four legs and] a support for the back.

rows.pali: Sattaṅgo nāma piṭṭhiapassayena ca ubhatopassesu apassayehi ca saddhiṃ kato.
rows.english: A “seven-limb” is one made with [four legs,] a support for the back and an [arm] support on each side.

rows.pali: Taṃ kira miḷābhayattherassa akaṃsu.
rows.english: They made that, it seems, for the Elder Pīṭhābhaya (Abhaya of the Chair).

rows.pali: Thero anāgāmī hutvā parinibbāyi.
rows.english: The elder became a non-returner, and then attained Nibbāna.

rows.pali: Imesaṃ pana tiṇṇampi seyyaṃ kappitamatte dhutaṅgaṃ bhijjati.
rows.english: As soon as any one of these three lies down, his ascetic practice is broken.

rows.pali: Ayamettha bhedo.
rows.english: This is the breach in this instance.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panānisaṃso, "seyyasukhaṃ passasukhaṃ middhasukhaṃ anuyutto viharatī"ti (dī. ni. 3.320; ma. ni. 1.186) vuttassa cetaso vinibandhassa upacchedanaṃ, sabbakammaṭṭhānānuyogasappāyatā, pāsādikairiyāpathatā, vīriyārambhānukūlatā, sammāpaṭipattiyā anubrūhananti.
rows.english: 75.The benefits are these. The mental shackle described thus, “He dwells indulging in the pleasure of lying prone, the pleasure of lolling, the pleasure of torpor” (M I 102), is severed; his state is suitable for devotion to any meditation subject; his deportment inspires confidence; his state favours the application of energy; he develops the right practice.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ nesajjikaṅge samādāna vidhānappabheda bhedānisaṃsavaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This is the commentary on the undertaking, directions, grades, breach, and benefits, in the case of the sitter’s practice.

rows.pali: 37.Idāni –
Imissā gāthāya vasena vaṇṇanā hoti.
rows.english: Now, there is the commentary according to the stanza (above).

rows.pali: Tattha kusalattikatoti sabbāneva hi dhutaṅgāni sekkhaputhujjanakhīṇāsavānaṃ vasena siyā kusalāni, siyā abyākatāni, natthi dhutaṅgaṃ akusalanti.
rows.english: 78. 4. Herein, as to the profitable triad: (Dhs, p.1) all the ascetic practices, that is to say, those of trainers, ordinary men, and men whose cankers have been destroyed, may be either profitable or [in the Arahant’s case] indeterminate. No ascetic practice is unprofitable.

rows.pali: Yo pana vadeyya "pāpiccho icchāpakato āraññiko hotīti ādivacanato (a. ni. 5.181; pari. 325) akusalampi dhutaṅga"nti.
rows.english: But if someone should say: There is also an unprofitable ascetic practice because of the words “One of evil wishes, a prey to wishes, becomes a forest dweller” (A III 219), etc.,

rows.pali: So vattabbo – na mayaṃ "akusalacittena araññe na vasatī"ti vadāma.
